meta.title: Swift & Objective-C
meta.description: Review checklist for iOS/macOS projects with Swift and Objective-C

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The pull request includes a summary explaining the change, the motivation behind it, and any relevant context such as linked tickets or design docs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A clear PR description helps reviewers understand intent quickly, reduces back-and-forth questions, and serves as future documentation when investigating why a change was made.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Read the PR description and check it explains the what and why
- Verify linked tickets or issues are referenced
- Confirm screenshots or recordings are included for UI changes
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add a brief description of why this refactor was needed? It'll help reviewers understand the motivation and make future archaeology easier.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
## PR Description
(empty)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
## PR Description
Refactors the NetworkManager to use async/await instead of completion handlers.

**Why:** Reduces callback nesting and prepares us for structured concurrency.
**Ticket:** MOBILE-1234
**Testing:** Unit tests updated, manual testing on iOS 17 simulator.

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Treat PR descriptions as documentation — they should explain intent, not just list file changes.

items.text: Is the change scoped appropriately, or should it be split into smaller PRs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR focuses on a single logical change rather than bundling multiple unrelated features, bug fixes, or refactors together.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large, multi-purpose PRs are harder to review, more likely to introduce bugs, and more difficult to revert if something goes wrong. Focused PRs get reviewed faster and more thoroughly.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if the PR touches unrelated modules or features
- Look for changes that could be submitted independently
- Verify the PR title accurately describes all changes (if it's hard to write a concise title, the PR may be too large)
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR includes both the new caching layer and the UI redesign of the settings screen. Could we split these into separate PRs? It would make each one easier to review and safer to merge.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// PR: "Update networking, add dark mode, fix crash"
// 47 files changed across 6 modules

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Each PR should represent one logical change that can be understood, reviewed, and reverted independently.

items.text: Are there any unrelated changes bundled in?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR does not include drive-by fixes, formatting changes, or refactors that are unrelated to the stated purpose of the change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unrelated changes obscure the actual intent of the PR, make code review harder, and complicate git blame history. They also increase the risk of the PR introducing unintended side effects.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Scan the diff for changes in files unrelated to the PR's purpose
- Look for whitespace-only or formatting-only changes mixed in
- Check for 'while I was here' fixes that could be separate PRs
baseContent.exampleComment: The formatting changes to `AppDelegate.swift` seem unrelated to the networking refactor. Could you move those to a separate PR so this one stays focused?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
// PR: "Fix login crash when user has no email"
// Only touches LoginViewController and LoginViewModelTests

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep PRs focused — submit unrelated changes as separate PRs to maintain clean review history.

items.text: Is dead code removed rather than commented out?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Unused code is deleted rather than commented out. Version control (git) preserves history, so there's no need to keep dead code as comments.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Commented-out code creates confusion about whether it's intentionally disabled, planned for restoration, or simply forgotten. It clutters the codebase and makes files harder to read.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for large blocks of commented-out code
- Check for functions or classes that are never called
- Look for #if false or #if 0 blocks wrapping unused code
baseContent.exampleComment: This commented-out `fetchLegacyUser()` method has been here since the migration. Since we can always recover it from git history, could we remove it to reduce noise?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Delete dead code instead of commenting it out — git is your backup.

items.text: Are TODO/FIXME comments accompanied by a tracking ticket?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TODO and FIXME comments include a reference to a tracking ticket (e.g., Jira, Linear, GitHub issue) so they can be prioritized and addressed systematically.
baseContent.whyItMatters: TODO comments without tickets tend to become permanent fixtures in the codebase. Linking them to tickets ensures they're triaged, assigned, and eventually resolved.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for TODO and FIXME comments in changed files
- Verify each has a ticket reference (e.g., MOBILE-1234)
- Check if the ticket actually exists and is in a relevant sprint or backlog
baseContent.exampleComment: This `// TODO: handle error` would benefit from a tracking ticket. Could you create one and reference it here so it doesn't get lost?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// TODO: handle this edge case
// FIXME: sometimes crashes here

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// TODO(MOBILE-1234): Handle expired token edge case
// FIXME(MOBILE-1235): Race condition in image cache — see ticket for repro steps

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every TODO/FIXME should have a ticket — if it's worth noting, it's worth tracking.

items.text: Does the commit history tell a coherent story?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The commits in the PR are logically structured, with each commit representing a meaningful step in the change. The history reads like a narrative that future developers can follow.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Clean commit history makes it easier to understand how a feature was built, bisect regressions, and review changes incrementally. Messy history with 'fix' and 'wip' commits obscures intent.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Read the commit messages in order — do they tell a story?
- Check for WIP, fixup, or typo-fix commits that should be squashed
- Verify each commit compiles and passes tests independently
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you squash the 'fix typo' and 'oops' commits into their parent commits? It'll make the history much cleaner for future readers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
abc1234 wip
def5678 more stuff
ghi9012 fix
jkl3456 oops
mno7890 actually fix

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
abc1234 Extract NetworkClient protocol
def5678 Implement URLSession-based NetworkClient
ghi9012 Migrate UserService to new NetworkClient
jkl3456 Add unit tests for NetworkClient

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Craft commit history that tells a clear story — each commit should be a meaningful, self-contained step.

items.text: Are types and protocols in PascalCase, properties and methods in camelCase?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Type names (classes, structs, enums, protocols) use PascalCase (e.g., `UserProfile`), while properties, methods, and local variables use camelCase (e.g., `userName`).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent casing is a fundamental Swift convention enforced by the Swift API Design Guidelines. Deviating from it makes code look foreign and harder to read for Swift developers.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that all new type declarations use PascalCase
- Verify properties, methods, and parameters use camelCase
- Look for any snake_case or ALL_CAPS (except for legacy Objective-C interop constants)
baseContent.exampleComment: The struct `user_profile` should be `UserProfile` per Swift naming conventions. Similarly, `GetUserName()` should be `getUserName()` or better yet, a computed property `userName`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow Swift conventions: PascalCase for types, camelCase for everything else.

items.text: Are function parameters labeled clearly following Swift API design guidelines?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Function parameter labels read naturally at the call site, forming grammatical phrases. External labels are used to clarify the role of each argument.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Swift's parameter labeling system is designed to make call sites read like English phrases. Poor labels force readers to look up function signatures to understand what arguments mean.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Read function calls aloud — do they form natural phrases?
- Check that external parameter labels clarify the argument's role
- Verify underscore `_` is used only when the label would be redundant
baseContent.exampleComment: At the call site, `move(p1, p2)` is unclear. Could we use `move(from: start, to: end)` so it reads naturally?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
func move(_ p1: Point, _ p2: Point) { }
move(startPoint, endPoint) // unclear what each arg means

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
func move(from start: Point, to end: Point) { }
move(from: startPoint, to: endPoint) // reads like English

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design parameter labels so call sites read as natural English phrases.

items.text: Are boolean properties/methods named as assertions (e.g., `isEmpty`, `hasContent`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Boolean properties and methods are named as assertions that read as true/false statements, using prefixes like `is`, `has`, `can`, `should`, or `allows`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Boolean names that read as assertions make conditions self-documenting. `if user.isActive` is immediately clear, while `if user.active` or `if user.status` requires context to understand.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that Boolean properties use assertive prefixes (is, has, can, should)
- Verify Boolean-returning methods also follow this pattern
- Look for ambiguous Boolean names like `flag`, `status`, or `check`
baseContent.exampleComment: The property `enabled` would be clearer as `isEnabled` — it reads better in conditions: `if button.isEnabled`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
var loading: Bool
var data: Bool
func check() -> Bool

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
var isLoading: Bool
var hasData: Bool
func canSubmit() -> Bool

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Name Booleans as assertions — they should read naturally in `if` statements.

items.text: Are abbreviations avoided in names unless universally understood?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Names use full words instead of abbreviations, except for widely understood terms like URL, ID, HTTP, JSON, or HTML.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Abbreviations save a few keystrokes but cost significantly more in readability. Code is read far more often than it's written, and unclear abbreviations force readers to guess or look up meanings.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Scan new names for non-standard abbreviations (e.g., `mgr`, `btn`, `vc`, `cfg`)
- Verify abbreviations used are universally understood (URL, ID, etc.)
- Check that abbreviations in uppercase follow Swift conventions (e.g., `urlString` not `URLString`)
baseContent.exampleComment: `usrMgr` is hard to parse at a glance. Could we use `userManager` instead? Swift favors clarity over brevity.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Spell out names fully — clarity beats brevity in Swift code.

items.text: Are factory methods named with `make` prefix per Swift conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Factory methods that create and return new instances use the `make` prefix (e.g., `makeIterator()`, `makeCoordinator()`), following Swift standard library conventions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The `make` prefix signals that the method creates a new instance each time it's called, distinguishing it from accessor methods that return existing objects. This convention is used throughout Swift's standard library.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that factory methods use the `make` prefix
- Verify that `create` or `build` prefixes are not used where `make` is conventional
- Look for methods returning new instances that lack the `make` prefix
baseContent.exampleComment: `createCoordinator()` should be `makeCoordinator()` per Swift conventions. The `make` prefix signals that a new instance is created each time.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
func createIterator() -> Iterator { ... }
func buildViewModel() -> ViewModel { ... }

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
func makeIterator() -> Iterator { ... }
func makeViewModel() -> ViewModel { ... }

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the `make` prefix for factory methods — it's a Swift standard library convention.

references.title: Swift API Design Guidelines — Strive for Fluent Usage

items.text: Are optionals unwrapped safely (guard let, if let, nil coalescing)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Optional values are unwrapped using safe patterns like `guard let`, `if let`, or nil coalescing (`??`) rather than force-unwrapping with `!`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Force-unwrapping a nil value causes a runtime crash. Safe unwrapping ensures the app handles missing values gracefully instead of crashing in production.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for `!` on optional values in changed code
- Verify each optional unwrap uses guard let, if let, or ??
- Check that force-unwraps, if any, have comments justifying them
baseContent.exampleComment: This force-unwrap `user!.name` will crash if `user` is nil. Could we use `guard let user` or optional chaining instead?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
let name = user!.name
let url = URL(string: urlString)!
print(dictionary["key"]!)

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always unwrap optionals safely — every `!` is a potential crash site.

items.text: Is force unwrapping (`!`) avoided unless the value is guaranteed non-nil?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Force-unwrapping (`!`) is used only when the value is provably non-nil — such as immediately after a nil check, with hardcoded values, or in test code — and never on values that could be nil at runtime.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Force-unwrapping is the #1 cause of crashes in Swift apps. Even when you 'know' a value exists, future code changes or unexpected states can make it nil, causing a crash.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Count force-unwraps in the diff
- For each one, verify the value is guaranteed non-nil at that point
- Check if a safe alternative (guard, if let, ??) would be better
baseContent.exampleComment: While `UIImage(named:)` rarely returns nil for bundled assets, it's safer to use `guard let` here since missing assets do happen during development.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
let image = UIImage(named: "icon")!  // crashes if asset missing
let data = try! JSONEncoder().encode(model)  // crashes on encoding failure

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Reserve force-unwrapping for provably non-nil values — use safe unwrapping everywhere else.

items.text: Are implicitly unwrapped optionals (`!`) justified and documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Implicitly unwrapped optionals (`var x: Type!`) are used only when a value is guaranteed to be set before use (e.g., IBOutlets, dependency injection) and have documentation explaining why.
baseContent.whyItMatters: IUOs bypass the compiler's nil safety, creating the same crash risk as force-unwrapping but hiding it in the type declaration. They should be rare and justified.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for `Type!` declarations in changed code
- Verify each IUO is justified (IBOutlets, two-phase init, etc.)
- Check that non-IBOutlet IUOs have comments explaining the guarantee
baseContent.exampleComment: This `var coordinator: Coordinator!` could be a regular optional with a `guard let` in the methods that use it. IUOs should be reserved for cases where the value is guaranteed to be set before access.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Limit IUOs to IBOutlets and two-phase initialization — prefer regular optionals or non-optional injection.

items.text: Are `as!` force casts avoided in favor of `as?` with handling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Type casts use `as?` (conditional cast) with proper nil handling instead of `as!` (forced cast) which crashes on failure.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Forced casts crash at runtime if the type doesn't match. Using `as?` lets you handle type mismatches gracefully with `guard let` or `if let`.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for `as!` in the diff
- Verify each forced cast is on a type that's guaranteed correct
- Check if `as?` with handling would be safer
baseContent.exampleComment: This `as! CustomCell` will crash if the cell type is wrong. Could we use `as?` with a guard and a descriptive assertion failure for debugging?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
let cell = tableView.dequeueReusableCell(withIdentifier: "Cell") as! CustomCell
let number = json["count"] as! Int

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `as?` with handling instead of `as!` — handle type mismatches instead of crashing.

items.text: Are enums with associated values used instead of loosely typed dictionaries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Enums with associated values are used to model distinct states or variants, instead of dictionaries or tuples with string keys that lose type safety.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Enums make invalid states unrepresentable and give you exhaustive switch checking. Dictionaries require runtime key checks and provide no compile-time guarantees about what keys exist.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for dictionaries used to represent state or variants
- Check for string-keyed lookups that model distinct cases
- Verify enum cases cover all states with appropriate associated data
baseContent.exampleComment: This `[String: Any]` response dictionary could be modeled as a `Result<User, APIError>` enum — it would make the success/failure states explicit and type-safe.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use enums with associated values to make states explicit and type-safe — avoid stringly-typed dictionaries.

items.text: Are generics used where they add value for reusability?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generic types and functions are used when code needs to work with multiple types while preserving type safety, but not over-applied when a concrete type suffices.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Generics enable code reuse without sacrificing type safety. However, premature or unnecessary generics add complexity. They should be used when there's a clear, current need for type flexibility.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if generic code is actually used with multiple types
- Verify generic constraints are specific enough (prefer protocol constraints over unconstrained generics)
- Look for cases where a concrete type would be simpler
baseContent.exampleComment: This generic `Repository<T>` is only ever used with `User`. Consider making it a concrete `UserRepository` until you actually need to support other types.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use generics when you need type-safe code reuse across multiple types — not as premature abstraction.

items.text: Are value types (struct) preferred over reference types (class) where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Structs (value types) are preferred over classes (reference types) for data models and simple types, reserving classes for when identity, inheritance, or reference semantics are needed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Value types are safer in concurrent code because each copy is independent — no shared mutable state. They also avoid retain cycle risks and are more predictable since mutations don't affect other references.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if new classes could be structs instead
- Verify classes are used only when inheritance, identity, or reference semantics are required
- Look for data models declared as classes that could be structs
baseContent.exampleComment: This `UserProfile` class doesn't use inheritance or reference semantics. Making it a struct would give us value semantics and eliminate retain cycle risks.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to structs for data types — use classes only when you need inheritance, identity, or reference semantics.

items.text: Are protocol-oriented patterns used instead of deep class hierarchies?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Shared behavior is defined through protocols and protocol extensions rather than deep class inheritance hierarchies, following Swift's protocol-oriented programming paradigm.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Deep inheritance creates tight coupling and fragile base class problems. Protocols enable composition over inheritance, allowing types to adopt multiple behaviors independently.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for class hierarchies deeper than 2-3 levels
- Look for base classes that could be replaced with protocols
- Verify protocol extensions provide default implementations where appropriate
baseContent.exampleComment: This 4-level class hierarchy (`BaseVC → ListVC → FilteredListVC → UserListVC`) is getting deep. Could we extract the filtering behavior into a protocol so it can be composed independently?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Favor protocols and composition over deep class hierarchies — it's the Swift way.

items.text: Are closures using `[weak self]` or `[unowned self]` to prevent retain cycles?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Closures that capture `self` use `[weak self]` or `[unowned self]` to avoid retain cycles, especially in escaping closures stored as properties or passed to long-lived objects.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Retain cycles are one of the most common memory leaks in Swift. When a closure captures `self` strongly and is stored on `self`, neither can be deallocated, leading to memory leaks.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check escaping closures for `self` captures without `[weak self]`
- Verify `[unowned self]` is used only when self is guaranteed to outlive the closure
- Look for stored closures (properties, completion handlers) that might create cycles
baseContent.exampleComment: This completion handler captures `self` strongly and is stored on the view model, creating a retain cycle. Adding `[weak self]` would prevent the memory leak.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `[weak self]` in escaping closures to prevent retain cycles — it's the default safe choice.

items.text: Are trailing closure syntax and shorthand argument names used appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Trailing closure syntax is used when the last parameter is a closure, and shorthand argument names (`$0`, `$1`) are used only in short, simple closures where meaning is obvious.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Trailing closures improve readability for common patterns like `map`, `filter`, and completion handlers. Shorthand arguments keep simple transforms concise, but in complex closures they obscure meaning.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that trailing closure syntax is used for the final closure argument
- Verify `$0` and `$1` are only used in closures of 1-2 lines
- Look for long closures using shorthand that would benefit from named parameters
baseContent.exampleComment: This multi-line closure using `$0` and `$1` is hard to follow. Could we use named parameters like `(user, index)` to make the intent clearer?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Shorthand in complex closure — hard to follow
users.sorted(by: { $0.department == $1.department ? $0.name < $1.name : $0.department < $1.department })

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use shorthand `$0` for simple one-liners; switch to named parameters when closures get complex.

items.text: Are `guard` statements used for early exits instead of nested `if` blocks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Guard statements are used to validate conditions and exit early, keeping the main logic at the lowest indentation level instead of nesting it inside multiple `if` blocks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Guard clauses reduce cognitive load by handling error cases upfront and keeping the happy path unindented. Deeply nested `if` blocks (the 'pyramid of doom') are harder to read and reason about.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for nested `if let` chains that could be flattened with `guard let`
- Check that guard is used for precondition validation at the start of functions
- Verify guard's else block always exits the scope (return, throw, continue, break)
baseContent.exampleComment: These nested `if let` blocks would be clearer as sequential `guard let` statements — it would flatten the code and keep the happy path at the top level.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use guard for early exits to keep the happy path at the lowest indentation level.

items.text: Is `defer` used for cleanup operations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The `defer` statement is used to ensure cleanup code runs when leaving a scope, regardless of how the scope is exited (return, throw, break).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without `defer`, cleanup code may be missed if a function has multiple exit points. `defer` guarantees resources are released, locks are unlocked, and files are closed.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for resource acquisition (file handles, locks, connections) without corresponding `defer` cleanup
- Check that `defer` is placed immediately after resource acquisition
- Verify cleanup code in functions with multiple return paths
baseContent.exampleComment: This file handle is opened but only closed in the success path. If the parsing throws, the handle leaks. A `defer { handle.close() }` right after opening would ensure cleanup.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `defer` immediately after resource acquisition to guarantee cleanup regardless of exit path.

items.text: Are `lazy` properties used for expensive initialization that may not be needed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Properties that are expensive to create or may not be accessed use the `lazy` keyword so they're initialized only on first access rather than during object initialization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Eager initialization of expensive properties (formatters, heavy views, database connections) wastes memory and slows startup if the property is never used. `lazy` defers the cost until needed.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for expensive property initializations in `init` or property declarations
- Check if properties like DateFormatters, NumberFormatters, or heavy views could be lazy
- Verify lazy properties are only used in single-threaded contexts (lazy is not thread-safe)
baseContent.exampleComment: This `DateFormatter` is created on init but only used when displaying dates. Making it `lazy` would avoid the initialization cost for views that don't show dates.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `lazy` for expensive properties that may not be accessed — defer initialization until first use.

items.text: Are computed properties used instead of getter methods?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Properties that derive their value from other properties use computed property syntax (`var x: Type { return ... }`) instead of getter methods (`func getX() -> Type`).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Swift's computed properties are more idiomatic than getter methods and provide a cleaner API. They also integrate with property wrappers, key paths, and SwiftUI's data flow.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for `getX()` or `fetchX()` methods that simply compute and return a value
- Check that they don't have side effects or expensive operations (which would be better as methods)
- Verify computed properties are O(1) or clearly documented if expensive
baseContent.exampleComment: The `getFullName()` method could be a computed property `fullName` — it's a pure derivation from `firstName` and `lastName` with no side effects.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use computed properties for derived values — reserve methods for operations with side effects or significant cost.

items.text: Are extensions used to organize code by protocol conformance?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Extensions are used to separate protocol conformance implementations into distinct blocks, organizing a type's code by concern rather than having everything in one large declaration.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Organizing conformances in extensions makes large types more navigable. You can quickly find all methods related to a specific protocol by looking at its extension block.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if protocol conformance methods are grouped in extensions
- Look for large type declarations that could benefit from extension-based organization
- Verify each extension has a `// MARK:` comment identifying the conformance
baseContent.exampleComment: The `UITableViewDataSource` methods are mixed into the main class body. Could we move them into an extension with a `// MARK: - UITableViewDataSource` header for better organization?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use extensions to organize protocol conformances — one extension per protocol with a MARK comment.

items.text: Are access modifiers (`private`, `fileprivate`, `internal`, `public`, `open`) used correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Access control modifiers are applied intentionally: `private` for implementation details, `internal` (default) for module-internal use, `public` for the module's API, and `open` when subclassing is intended.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Proper access control enforces encapsulation, reduces API surface area, and prevents external code from depending on implementation details. It also enables compiler optimizations for `private` and `fileprivate`.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that helper methods and properties are `private` if only used within the type
- Verify `public` is only applied to intentional API surfaces
- Look for missing access modifiers on types that should be `public` in library/framework code
baseContent.exampleComment: The `parseResponse()` helper is only called within this class. Marking it `private` would prevent other types from accidentally depending on it.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Start with the most restrictive access level (`private`) and only widen when there's a clear need.

items.text: Is `@discardableResult` used when function return values are intentionally ignorable?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functions whose return values can reasonably be ignored are annotated with `@discardableResult`, while functions whose return values should always be used generate a compiler warning when ignored.
baseContent.whyItMatters: By default, Swift warns when a function's return value is unused. `@discardableResult` suppresses this for functions where ignoring the result is a valid use case, while keeping the warning for functions where the result matters.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for `@discardableResult` annotations and verify they're appropriate
- Look for `_ =` assignments that suppress compiler warnings — the function might need `@discardableResult`
- Verify functions that return important values (errors, success status) are NOT marked `@discardableResult`
baseContent.exampleComment: The `save()` method returns a `Bool` indicating success, which callers should check. Removing `@discardableResult` would warn callers who accidentally ignore the result.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `@discardableResult` only when ignoring the return value is a valid use case — not to silence warnings.

items.text: Are higher-order functions (map, filter, reduce, compactMap) used where clearer than loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Higher-order functions like `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, and `compactMap` are used when they make the intent clearer than equivalent `for` loops, but not when they hurt readability.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Functional transforms express intent declaratively — `names.filter { $0.isActive }` instantly communicates 'get active names.' But chaining too many transforms or using them for side effects obscures intent.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that functional transforms are used for simple, pure transformations
- Verify `for` loops are used when the operation has side effects or complex logic
- Look for deeply chained transforms that could be clearer as a loop
baseContent.exampleComment: This chain of `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `compactMap` is hard to follow. Could we break it into named intermediate steps or use a `for` loop for clarity?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Over-chained — hard to follow
let result = data.map { $0.items }.flatMap { $0 }.filter { $0.isValid }.compactMap { $0.transform() }.reduce(0) { $0 + $1.value }

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use functional transforms for simple, declarative operations — switch to loops when logic gets complex.

items.text: Is `compactMap` used instead of `map` + `filter { $0 != nil }` for optional unwrapping?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `compactMap` is used to transform and unwrap optionals in one step, instead of the less efficient `map { }.filter { $0 != nil }.map { $0! }` pattern.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `compactMap` is purpose-built for this operation — it's more concise, eliminates force-unwrapping, and makes the intent clear: 'transform and keep only non-nil results.'
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for `.filter { $0 != nil }` or `.map { $0! }` patterns
- Check for `flatMap` used for optional unwrapping (deprecated in favor of `compactMap`)
- Verify `compactMap` is used when both transforming and filtering nils
baseContent.exampleComment: This `map + filter + force-unwrap` chain can be simplified to a single `compactMap` — it's cleaner and eliminates the force unwrap.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
let urls = strings
    .map { URL(string: $0) }      // [URL?]
    .filter { $0 != nil }          // [URL?] still optional
    .map { $0! }                   // [URL] force unwrap

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: let urls = strings.compactMap { URL(string: $0) }  // [URL]

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `compactMap` to transform and unwrap optionals in one step — no force unwrapping needed.

items.text: Are `Set` and `Dictionary` used instead of arrays for lookup-heavy operations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When code frequently checks membership or looks up values by key, `Set` or `Dictionary` are used instead of `Array` to get O(1) lookups instead of O(n).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Searching an array with `contains` or `first(where:)` is O(n). For collections that are searched frequently, converting to a Set or Dictionary provides O(1) lookups, which can be a massive performance win.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for `array.contains()` or `array.first(where:)` in loops or hot paths
- Check if collections used for lookups could be Sets or Dictionaries
- Verify Set is used when order doesn't matter and elements are Hashable
baseContent.exampleComment: This `blockedUserIds.contains(id)` is called in a loop for every message. Since `blockedUserIds` is an Array, each check is O(n). Converting to a `Set` would make it O(1).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Set for membership checks and Dictionary for key-based lookups — Array.contains() is O(n).

items.text: Is `reduce` avoided when it hurts readability?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `reduce` is used only for simple accumulations (sums, counts, building strings). Complex or multi-step logic in `reduce` closures is refactored into clearer `for` loops.
baseContent.whyItMatters: While `reduce` is powerful, complex reduce closures are notoriously hard to read and debug. A `for` loop with named variables is often dramatically clearer for non-trivial accumulations.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check `reduce` closures for length — more than 2-3 lines is a code smell
- Verify the accumulation logic is simple (sum, concatenation, counting)
- Look for `reduce(into:)` usage for better performance with value types
baseContent.exampleComment: This `reduce` closure builds a complex nested dictionary over 15 lines. A `for` loop with named intermediate variables would be much easier to understand and debug.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Complex reduce — hard to follow
let grouped = items.reduce(into: [String: [String: [Item]]]()) { result, item in
    var category = result[item.category] ?? [:]
    var subcategory = category[item.subcategory] ?? []
    subcategory.append(item)
    category[item.subcategory] = subcategory
    result[item.category] = category
}

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep `reduce` simple — if the closure exceeds 2-3 lines, a `for` loop is probably clearer.

items.text: Are `@objc` annotations used only where Objective-C interop is required?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `@objc` annotations are applied only to methods, properties, and classes that need to be visible to the Objective-C runtime — not applied broadly or unnecessarily.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnecessary `@objc` annotations expose Swift code to the Objective-C runtime, increasing binary size, slowing method dispatch, and preventing Swift-only optimizations. They should be used intentionally.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that `@objc` is only on methods used by selectors, KVO, Interface Builder, or Objective-C code
- Look for `@objcMembers` on classes — it exposes all members, which is usually too broad
- Verify `@objc` isn't used just to suppress compiler errors (which may indicate a design issue)
baseContent.exampleComment: This class uses `@objcMembers` but only one method needs `@objc` for the selector. Could we remove `@objcMembers` and add `@objc` only to `handleTap()`?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Apply `@objc` surgically — only where Objective-C interop is actually needed.

items.text: Are nullability annotations (`nullable`, `nonnull`, `NS_ASSUME_NONNULL_BEGIN`) used in ObjC headers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Objective-C header files use nullability annotations (`nullable`, `nonnull`, `NS_ASSUME_NONNULL_BEGIN/END`) to specify which pointers can be nil, enabling better Swift interop.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without nullability annotations, all Objective-C pointers import into Swift as implicitly unwrapped optionals (`Type!`), losing type safety. Annotations make the Swift interface use proper optionals or non-optionals.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check Objective-C headers for `NS_ASSUME_NONNULL_BEGIN`/`END` wrapping
- Verify nullable parameters and return values are marked `nullable`
- Look at the Swift-generated interface to confirm optionality is correct
baseContent.exampleComment: This header is missing nullability annotations, so all properties import as implicitly unwrapped optionals in Swift. Adding `NS_ASSUME_NONNULL_BEGIN` and marking nullable params would improve the Swift API.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add nullability annotations to all Objective-C headers for proper Swift optional/non-optional bridging.

items.text: Are ObjC bridging headers minimal and maintained?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The Objective-C bridging header only imports what's needed for Swift code to interact with Objective-C, and unused imports are removed to keep it clean.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A bloated bridging header increases compile times because every import is processed for every Swift file in the module. It also exposes more of the Objective-C API surface than necessary.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check the bridging header for unused imports
- Verify each import is actually referenced from Swift code
- Look for opportunities to use module maps or framework imports instead
baseContent.exampleComment: The bridging header imports 15 Objective-C headers but only 3 are referenced from Swift code. Removing the unused imports would improve compile times.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// ProjectName-Bridging-Header.h
#import "LegacyNetworking.h"
#import "OldDatabase.h"       // no longer used from Swift
#import "DeprecatedUtils.h"   // no longer used from Swift
#import "Analytics.h"
#import "CrashReporter.h"     // no longer used from Swift

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep the bridging header minimal — only import Objective-C headers that Swift code actually uses.

items.text: Are ObjC categories/extensions named to avoid method conflicts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Objective-C categories use prefixed method names (e.g., `abc_customMethod`) to avoid collisions with Apple's private methods or other categories on the same class.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Objective-C categories can silently override existing methods, including Apple's private implementations. This causes unpredictable behavior and App Store rejections. Prefixing prevents collisions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that category methods use a unique prefix
- Verify category file names follow the `ClassName+CategoryName` convention
- Look for generic method names in categories that might conflict with system methods
baseContent.exampleComment: The category method `isValid` on `NSString` could conflict with a private Apple method. Could we prefix it as `myapp_isValid` to avoid potential collisions?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always prefix Objective-C category methods to avoid silent method collisions.

items.text: Is ARC (Automatic Reference Counting) used correctly with no manual retain/release?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code uses ARC (Automatic Reference Counting) and doesn't contain manual `retain`, `release`, or `autorelease` calls. ARC-incompatible patterns like manual memory management are avoided.
baseContent.whyItMatters: ARC handles reference counting automatically, and mixing manual memory management creates over-release crashes or leaks. Modern Objective-C code should always use ARC.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for manual `retain`, `release`, `autorelease` calls
- Check that `-fno-objc-arc` compiler flag isn't applied to new files
- Verify `dealloc` doesn't call `[super dealloc]` (ARC does this automatically)
baseContent.exampleComment: This file calls `[object release]` manually. Since the project uses ARC, these calls will cause over-release crashes. ARC handles this automatically — just remove the manual calls.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Let ARC manage memory — never use manual retain/release in ARC-enabled code.

items.text: Are `NS_ENUM` and `NS_OPTIONS` used for enumerations in ObjC?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Objective-C enumerations use the `NS_ENUM` and `NS_OPTIONS` macros instead of plain C enums, enabling better type safety and Swift interoperability.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `NS_ENUM` creates a proper typed enum that bridges to Swift as a native enum. Plain C enums import as loose integer constants, losing type safety and switch exhaustiveness checking.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that new enums use `NS_ENUM` for mutually exclusive values
- Verify `NS_OPTIONS` is used for bitmask/flag enumerations
- Look for plain `enum` declarations that should be converted
baseContent.exampleComment: This plain C enum won't bridge cleanly to Swift. Using `NS_ENUM(NSInteger, UserStatus)` would create a proper Swift enum with exhaustive switching.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `NS_ENUM` for exclusive enumerations and `NS_OPTIONS` for bitmask flags — they bridge correctly to Swift.

items.text: Are `instancetype` return types used instead of `id` for init and factory methods?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Objective-C init and factory methods return `instancetype` instead of `id`, enabling the compiler to infer the correct return type and catch type errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `instancetype` tells the compiler the return type matches the receiver's class, enabling type checking that `id` completely bypasses. This catches bugs at compile time rather than runtime.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that `init` methods return `instancetype`
- Verify factory methods (e.g., `+ (instancetype)managerWithConfig:`) use `instancetype`
- Look for methods returning `id` that should return `instancetype`
baseContent.exampleComment: This factory method returns `id`, which loses type information. Returning `instancetype` would enable the compiler to catch type mismatches.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `instancetype` instead of `id` for init and factory methods to enable compile-time type checking.

items.text: Is property memory semantics correct (`strong`, `weak`, `copy`, `assign`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Objective-C property attributes (`strong`, `weak`, `copy`, `assign`) are chosen correctly based on ownership and mutability semantics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong memory semantics cause crashes or leaks: a `strong` delegate creates retain cycles, `assign` for objects causes dangling pointer crashes, and missing `copy` on `NSString` allows mutation through the original reference.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Verify delegates are `weak` to prevent retain cycles
- Check `NSString` and `NSArray` properties use `copy` (prevents mutation through original mutable reference)
- Confirm block properties use `copy`
- Verify `assign` is only used for primitive types (NSInteger, BOOL, CGFloat)
baseContent.exampleComment: This `NSString` property uses `strong` instead of `copy`. If someone passes an `NSMutableString`, they could mutate it after assignment. Using `copy` prevents this.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `weak` for delegates, `copy` for value-semantic types (NSString, NSArray, blocks), `strong` for owned objects, `assign` for primitives.

items.text: Are retain cycles prevented (weak references in closures, delegate patterns)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Object graphs don't form strong reference cycles. Closures use `[weak self]`, delegates are declared `weak`, and parent-child relationships use weak back-references.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Retain cycles are the most common memory leak in Swift/ObjC. When two objects hold strong references to each other, neither can be deallocated, causing memory to grow indefinitely.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check closures stored as properties for `[weak self]` captures
- Verify delegate properties are `weak`
- Look for parent-child relationships where the child holds a strong ref to parent
- Run Instruments Leaks on suspicious flows
baseContent.exampleComment: The `onComplete` closure captures `self` strongly and is stored on `self.viewModel`, creating a retain cycle. Adding `[weak self]` would break the cycle.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Break retain cycles with `weak` references — delegates, parent back-references, and closure captures are the usual suspects.

items.text: Are delegates declared as `weak` to prevent retain cycles?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Delegate properties are declared as `weak var` to avoid retain cycles between the delegating object and its delegate (typically a view controller that owns the delegating object).
baseContent.whyItMatters: A strong delegate reference creates a retain cycle: the owner holds the delegating object strongly, and the delegating object holds the owner (delegate) strongly. Neither can be deallocated.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that all delegate properties are `weak var`
- Verify the delegate protocol is `AnyObject` or class-bound (required for `weak`)
- Look for protocol declarations missing `: AnyObject` constraint
baseContent.exampleComment: This `delegate` property isn't `weak`, which will create a retain cycle with the owning view controller. Could we make it `weak var delegate: ProfileDelegate?`?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always declare delegate properties as `weak` and constrain the protocol to `AnyObject`.

items.text: Is `autoreleasepool` used for memory-intensive loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `autoreleasepool` blocks wrap iterations in loops that create many temporary objects, allowing those objects to be released at the end of each iteration instead of accumulating.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In tight loops creating temporary objects (image processing, file parsing, data transformation), objects pile up in memory until the pool drains at the end of the run loop. Wrapping iterations in `autoreleasepool` keeps peak memory low.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for loops processing large datasets (images, files, records)
- Check if temporary objects accumulate during iteration
- Verify `autoreleasepool` wraps the loop body, not the entire loop
baseContent.exampleComment: This loop processes 10,000 images without an autoreleasepool. Each iteration's temporary objects accumulate until the loop ends, spiking memory. Wrapping the body in `autoreleasepool` would keep memory stable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Wrap memory-intensive loop bodies in `autoreleasepool` to release temporary objects each iteration.

items.text: Are large resources (images, data) released when no longer needed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Large objects like images, data buffers, and caches are explicitly released (set to nil or removed from collections) when they're no longer needed, not held indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: iOS has strict memory limits and will terminate apps that exceed them. Holding large resources longer than needed leads to memory warnings and termination, especially on older devices.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that image caches have size limits and eviction policies
- Verify large data buffers are nil'd after processing
- Look for `didReceiveMemoryWarning` or notification handlers that clear caches
- Check view controllers release resources in `viewDidDisappear` if appropriate
baseContent.exampleComment: This full-resolution image is kept in memory even after being displayed as a thumbnail. Could we release the full image after generating the thumbnail?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Release large resources proactively — use caches with limits and respond to memory warnings.

items.text: Are Instruments (Leaks, Allocations) findings addressed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Memory issues identified by Xcode Instruments (Leaks, Allocations, Memory Graph Debugger) are investigated and resolved before the PR is merged.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Instruments provides concrete evidence of memory problems. Ignoring leak warnings leads to growing memory usage, degraded performance, and eventual app termination by the OS.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Ask if Instruments was run on the changed code paths
- Check for known leak patterns (retain cycles, observer registration without removal)
- Verify the Memory Graph Debugger shows no unexpected object retention
baseContent.exampleComment: Have we profiled this with Instruments Leaks? The closure-based notification pattern here looks like it could create a retain cycle that Instruments would catch.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Profile with Instruments before merging memory-sensitive code — fix all leaks and unbounded growth.

items.text: Is `deinit` implemented to clean up observations, timers, and subscriptions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `deinit` is implemented in classes that register for notifications, create timers, or hold subscriptions, to ensure these are cleaned up when the object is deallocated.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaked timers continue firing after their owner is deallocated, notifications are delivered to stale objects causing crashes, and Combine subscriptions hold strong references preventing deallocation.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for `NotificationCenter.addObserver` without corresponding removal in `deinit`
- Look for `Timer.scheduledTimer` without `invalidate()` in `deinit`
- Verify Combine subscriptions use `store(in: &cancellables)` with cancellables cleared in `deinit`
baseContent.exampleComment: This view controller registers for `UIKeyboardWillShow` but never removes the observer. Adding a `deinit` that calls `NotificationCenter.removeObserver(self)` would prevent crashes after deallocation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Implement `deinit` to clean up timers, observers, and subscriptions — prevent leaks and crashes.

items.text: Are Swift concurrency features (async/await, actors) used instead of GCD where available?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New code uses Swift's structured concurrency (async/await, actors, task groups) instead of Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) completion handlers, where the deployment target supports it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Swift concurrency is safer, more readable, and compiler-checked. It eliminates callback hell, prevents data races with actors, and enables structured cancellation — all of which are error-prone with GCD.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if new async code uses `async/await` instead of `DispatchQueue` + completion handlers
- Verify the deployment target supports Swift concurrency (iOS 13+ with back-deployment, iOS 15+ native)
- Look for opportunities to convert completion handler APIs to async wrappers
baseContent.exampleComment: Since we target iOS 15+, this GCD-based fetch could use async/await. It would eliminate the completion handler nesting and make error handling linear.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer async/await over GCD for new code — it's safer, cleaner, and compiler-verified.

items.text: Is `@MainActor` used for UI-related code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: UI-related classes and methods are annotated with `@MainActor` to ensure they always run on the main thread, preventing UI updates from background threads.
baseContent.whyItMatters: UIKit and SwiftUI require all UI updates on the main thread. Without `@MainActor`, async code might update UI from a background thread, causing crashes, visual glitches, or undefined behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that view models and UI-updating code are marked `@MainActor`
- Verify `@MainActor` is on the class/struct level or individual methods as appropriate
- Look for `DispatchQueue.main.async` that could be replaced with `@MainActor`
baseContent.exampleComment: This view model updates `@Published` properties from an async context without `@MainActor`. SwiftUI will crash or behave unpredictably. Adding `@MainActor` to the class ensures all updates happen on the main thread.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `@MainActor` on view models and UI code to guarantee main-thread execution.

items.text: Are data races prevented (actors, locks, serial queues)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Shared mutable state is protected from concurrent access using actors, locks, or serial dispatch queues. Multiple threads don't read and write the same data simultaneously.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Data races cause crashes, corrupted data, and impossible-to-reproduce bugs. They're one of the hardest bug categories to diagnose and fix, making prevention essential.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for mutable properties accessed from multiple threads without synchronization
- Verify actors are used for shared state in async contexts
- Look for `var` properties on classes accessed concurrently without locks
baseContent.exampleComment: This `cache` dictionary is accessed from multiple async tasks without synchronization. Wrapping it in an actor would prevent data races.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use actors to protect shared mutable state — they make data races a compile-time error.

items.text: Are `Task` cancellation and cooperative cancellation handled?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Async tasks check for cancellation using `Task.checkCancellation()` or `Task.isCancelled` and clean up appropriately when cancelled, rather than running to completion unnecessarily.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without cancellation handling, tasks continue running even when their results are no longer needed (e.g., user navigated away). This wastes resources and can cause bugs when updating stale state.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that long-running async operations check `Task.isCancelled`
- Verify tasks stored as properties are cancelled when no longer needed (e.g., in `deinit` or `onDisappear`)
- Look for `Task.checkCancellation()` in loops and before expensive operations
baseContent.exampleComment: This search task isn't cancelled when the user types a new query, so stale results might arrive after current ones. Storing and cancelling the task would fix this.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cancel obsolete tasks and check for cancellation in long-running operations to avoid stale updates.

items.text: Is `DispatchQueue.main` used for UI updates when not using async/await?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When using GCD (not async/await), UI updates from background operations are dispatched to the main queue with `DispatchQueue.main.async`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: UIKit is not thread-safe. Updating UI from background threads causes visual glitches, crashes, and undefined behavior. The main queue is the only safe place for UI operations.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check completion handlers called from background queues for UI updates
- Verify `DispatchQueue.main.async` wraps any UI changes in callbacks
- Look for URLSession completion handlers (which run on background threads by default)
baseContent.exampleComment: This URLSession completion handler updates `self.label.text` directly, but URLSession callbacks run on a background thread. Wrapping the UI update in `DispatchQueue.main.async` would fix this.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always dispatch UI updates to the main queue when working with GCD and completion handlers.

items.text: Are `Sendable` conformances correct for types shared across concurrency domains?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Types passed between concurrency domains (across actor boundaries, into tasks) conform to `Sendable` to guarantee they can be safely shared without data races.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Swift's `Sendable` protocol is a compile-time guarantee that a type is safe to share across concurrency domains. Non-Sendable types crossing boundaries can cause data races.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for `Sendable` conformance on types passed to actors or across task boundaries
- Verify structs with only value-type stored properties conform to `Sendable`
- Look for `@unchecked Sendable` and verify the thread-safety guarantees are actually met
baseContent.exampleComment: This `Config` class is passed into a `Task` but isn't `Sendable`. Since it's a mutable class, concurrent access could cause data races. Could we make it a struct or add proper synchronization?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Conform to `Sendable` for types shared across concurrency domains — prefer value types for natural Sendability.

items.text: Are completion handlers replaced with async/await where Swift concurrency is available?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Legacy completion-handler APIs are wrapped with async/await interfaces using `withCheckedContinuation` or `withCheckedThrowingContinuation`, providing a modern async API.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Completion handlers lead to nested callbacks, forgotten completions, and error handling spread across closures. Async wrappers provide linear, readable, try/catch-based flow.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for new completion-handler APIs that could be async instead
- Verify continuations are resumed exactly once (not zero or multiple times)
- Look for existing completion-handler APIs that have async equivalents in newer SDKs
baseContent.exampleComment: Since we support iOS 15+, we can use `URLSession.data(from:)` directly instead of wrapping `dataTask` in a continuation. Apple provides async versions of most system APIs.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Wrap legacy completion handlers with `withCheckedContinuation` to provide clean async/await interfaces.

items.text: Is `TaskGroup` or `async let` used for concurrent operations instead of nested callbacks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Concurrent operations use `async let` for a fixed number of parallel tasks or `TaskGroup` for a dynamic number, instead of nested completion handlers or manual dispatch groups.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `async let` and `TaskGroup` provide structured concurrency with automatic cancellation, error propagation, and clear parent-child relationships. Nested callbacks lack these guarantees.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for multiple independent async calls that could run concurrently with `async let`
- Look for loops spawning concurrent tasks that should use `TaskGroup`
- Verify DispatchGroup usage could be replaced with structured concurrency
baseContent.exampleComment: These three sequential network calls are independent and could use `async let` to run concurrently, cutting load time from ~3 seconds to ~1 second.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `async let` for fixed concurrent tasks and `TaskGroup` for dynamic ones — both provide structured concurrency.

items.text: Are view controllers not overly large (Massive View Controller anti-pattern)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: View controllers focus on coordinating the view lifecycle and delegating business logic to separate objects (view models, coordinators, services) rather than containing everything.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Massive View Controllers (MVC = Massive View Controller) are the most common iOS anti-pattern. They're hard to test, hard to modify, and tend to grow unboundedly as features are added.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check view controller line count — above 300-400 lines is a warning sign
- Verify networking, data transformation, and business logic are in separate types
- Look for table/collection view data source methods doing heavy data processing inline
baseContent.exampleComment: This view controller is 800 lines with networking, data parsing, and UI logic all mixed in. Could we extract the networking into a service and the data processing into a view model?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep view controllers thin — delegate business logic, networking, and data processing to separate objects.

items.text: Are `@State`, `@Binding`, `@ObservedObject`, `@StateObject` used correctly in SwiftUI?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SwiftUI property wrappers (`@State`, `@Binding`, `@ObservedObject`, `@StateObject`, `@EnvironmentObject`) are chosen based on ownership and lifetime semantics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using the wrong property wrapper causes bugs: `@ObservedObject` for owned state causes recreation on redraws, `@State` for reference types doesn't trigger updates, and `@Binding` without a source of truth creates stale data.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Verify `@StateObject` is used for objects the view creates and owns
- Check `@ObservedObject` is used for objects injected from a parent
- Confirm `@State` is used for simple value-type view-local state
- Look for `@Binding` with a matching `@State` source of truth in a parent view
baseContent.exampleComment: This view creates the `ViewModel` inline with `@ObservedObject`. This means it's recreated every time the parent redraws. Use `@StateObject` since this view owns the object.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `@StateObject` for owned objects and `@ObservedObject` for injected ones — never create objects inline with `@ObservedObject`.

references.title: Managing Model Data in Your App

items.text: Is `@StateObject` used for owned objects and `@ObservedObject` for injected ones?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `@StateObject` initializes and owns an `ObservableObject`, while `@ObservedObject` receives one from a parent view. The distinction determines the object's lifetime.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `@StateObject` persists across view redraws — it's created once and survives. `@ObservedObject` doesn't own the object, so if used to create one, SwiftUI may recreate it on every redraw, losing state.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that views creating their own ObservableObjects use `@StateObject`
- Verify views receiving ObservableObjects from parents use `@ObservedObject`
- Look for `@ObservedObject var vm = ViewModel()` — this is almost always wrong
baseContent.exampleComment: This `@ObservedObject var vm = ViewModel()` will be recreated every time the parent redraws. Since this view owns the object, it should be `@StateObject`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: `@StateObject` = you create it, `@ObservedObject` = someone gives it to you.

items.text: Are `ObservableObject` publishers minimal to prevent unnecessary view updates?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `@Published` properties on `ObservableObject` classes are minimized, and unrelated state is split into separate objects to prevent unnecessary view re-renders.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every `@Published` property change triggers all observing views to re-evaluate their `body`. A view model with 10 `@Published` properties causes re-renders even when only one changes, degrading performance.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Count `@Published` properties — more than 5-6 is a warning sign
- Check if unrelated properties could be split into separate observable objects
- Verify views only observe the data they actually display
baseContent.exampleComment: This view model has 12 `@Published` properties, but the list view only uses `items` and `isLoading`. Splitting into focused view models would prevent the list from re-rendering when unrelated properties change.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Split large ObservableObjects into focused ones — each @Published change triggers re-renders of all observers.

items.text: Is the view hierarchy structured to minimize unnecessary SwiftUI redraws?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The SwiftUI view hierarchy is structured so that state changes only trigger re-evaluation of the minimal set of views, using techniques like extracting subviews and moving state down.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SwiftUI re-evaluates a view's `body` whenever its state or observed data changes. If state is held too high in the hierarchy, large portions of the tree re-render unnecessarily.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that `@State` is placed on the lowest view that needs it
- Verify complex views are broken into smaller subviews
- Look for state in parent views that only affects one child
baseContent.exampleComment: The `isExpanded` state is on the list view, but it only affects one row. Moving it into the row subview would prevent the entire list from re-rendering on expand/collapse.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Push state down to the lowest view that needs it to minimize re-render scope.

items.text: Are storyboard/XIB changes minimal and not causing merge conflicts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Storyboard and XIB file changes are minimal, targeted, and avoid unnecessary diffs caused by Xcode regenerating internal IDs or reordering XML elements.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Storyboard/XIB files are XML that Xcode frequently reformats, creating massive diffs that are impossible to review meaningfully and cause constant merge conflicts in team environments.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check the storyboard/XIB diff size — large diffs for small changes indicate Xcode reformatting
- Verify only the intended views/constraints were changed
- Look for ID regeneration or element reordering that inflates the diff
baseContent.exampleComment: This storyboard diff is 500 lines but you only added one label. It looks like Xcode reformatted the entire file. Could you revert the unrelated changes and only commit the label addition?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
<!-- 500-line diff for adding one label -->
<!-- Xcode reformatted all element ordering and regenerated IDs -->

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
Strategies to minimize storyboard conflicts:
1. Use separate storyboards per feature/flow
2. Consider programmatic UI or SwiftUI for complex screens
3. Open storyboard only when editing, close immediately
4. Use storyboard references for cross-flow navigation

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Minimize storyboard diffs — consider programmatic UI or SwiftUI for complex or frequently-changed screens.

items.text: Are Auto Layout constraints correct and not causing ambiguous layouts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Auto Layout constraints fully define the position and size of every view without conflicts (over-constrained) or ambiguity (under-constrained), and don't generate runtime warnings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Ambiguous layouts cause unpredictable view positioning that varies across devices. Conflicting constraints generate console warnings and force the system to break constraints arbitrarily.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check the console for "Unable to simultaneously satisfy constraints" warnings
- Verify each view has enough constraints to determine x, y, width, and height
- Look for hardcoded frame values mixed with Auto Layout
- Test on multiple screen sizes
baseContent.exampleComment: This view has horizontal constraints but no vertical positioning — Auto Layout will guess where to place it vertically. Could you add top/bottom constraints?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
label.translatesAutoresizingMaskIntoConstraints = false
NSLayoutConstraint.activate([
    label.topAnchor.constraint(equalTo: headerView.bottomAnchor, constant: 12),
    label.leadingAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.leadingAnchor, constant: 16),
    label.trailingAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.trailingAnchor, constant: -16)
    // height determined by intrinsic content size
])

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Ensure every view has unambiguous, non-conflicting constraints — check the console for Auto Layout warnings.

items.text: Are custom views reusable and following single responsibility?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom views handle one responsibility (displaying a specific piece of UI), are configurable via properties, and don't contain business logic or networking.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Views that mix display, data fetching, and business logic are impossible to reuse and hard to test. Single-responsibility views can be composed into different screens and tested in isolation.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that custom views don't make network calls or access databases
- Verify views are configured via properties/init parameters, not internal data fetching
- Look for views that could be used in multiple places but are too specialized
baseContent.exampleComment: This `UserCard` view fetches user data internally. If it instead received a `User` model via init, it could be reused in the search results, profile, and contacts screens.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Views should display data, not fetch it — pass data in, keep views reusable and testable.

items.text: Is accessibility supported (VoiceOver labels, dynamic type, color contrast)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: UI components support accessibility features: VoiceOver labels for interactive elements, Dynamic Type for text scaling, and sufficient color contrast ratios.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Accessibility is both a legal requirement and a quality standard. Apps that don't support VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, and color contrast exclude users with disabilities and may violate accessibility laws.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that interactive elements have `accessibilityLabel` set
- Verify text uses Dynamic Type fonts (preferredFont or @ScaledMetric)
- Test with VoiceOver enabled
- Check color contrast ratios meet WCAG AA standards (4.5:1 for text)
baseContent.exampleComment: This custom button uses a fixed font size of 14pt, which won't scale with Dynamic Type. Using `.preferredFont(forTextStyle: .body)` or `@ScaledMetric` would make it accessible.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add VoiceOver labels, use Dynamic Type fonts, and ensure sufficient contrast — accessibility is a requirement, not a feature.

items.text: Are images using asset catalogs with appropriate scale variants?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Images are stored in Xcode Asset Catalogs with proper 1x, 2x, and 3x scale variants, and use platform-specific assets where needed (dark mode, device-specific).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Asset catalogs optimize app size (app thinning delivers only the relevant scale), provide automatic dark mode support, and ensure crisp images on all device resolutions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that new images are added to an asset catalog, not loose in the project
- Verify all three scale variants (1x, 2x, 3x) are provided
- Look for hardcoded image paths that should use `UIImage(named:)` or SwiftUI `Image()`
baseContent.exampleComment: This image is added as a loose PNG file. Moving it to the asset catalog would enable app thinning, dark mode variants, and proper scaling across devices.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Loose file — no scale variants, no app thinning
let image = UIImage(contentsOfFile: Bundle.main.path(forResource: "icon", ofType: "png")!)

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Asset Catalogs for all images — they enable app thinning, dark mode, and automatic scale selection.

items.text: Are errors modeled with Swift `Error` enums with descriptive cases?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Errors are defined as enums conforming to `Error` (or `LocalizedError`) with descriptive cases that carry relevant context, rather than using generic error types or string messages.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Typed error enums enable exhaustive `catch` handling, carry structured context (user ID, status code), and provide better debugging information than generic `NSError` or string-based errors.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that custom errors use `enum: Error` with descriptive cases
- Verify error cases include associated values for context (e.g., status codes, failed IDs)
- Look for `NSError` or `Error` used generically where typed errors would be better
baseContent.exampleComment: This function throws a generic `NSError` with a string message. Defining a `NetworkError` enum with cases like `.unauthorized`, `.notFound(id:)` would make error handling more precise.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Model errors as enums with descriptive cases and associated values — enable precise, exhaustive error handling.

items.text: Are `do-catch` blocks handling specific error cases, not just generic `catch`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `do-catch` blocks catch specific error types and cases, handling each appropriately, rather than catching all errors with a generic `catch` that logs or ignores them.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A generic `catch` treats all errors identically — a network timeout gets the same handling as an authentication failure. Specific catches enable appropriate recovery: retry for timeouts, re-login for auth errors.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that `catch` blocks handle specific error types or cases
- Verify the generic `catch` at the bottom handles truly unexpected errors
- Look for empty catch blocks or `catch { }` that silently swallow errors
baseContent.exampleComment: This generic `catch { print(error) }` treats network errors, parsing errors, and auth errors identically. Could we catch specific cases to provide appropriate recovery (retry, re-login, etc.)?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Catch specific error types to enable appropriate recovery — generic catches hide distinct failure modes.

items.text: Are `try?` and `try!` used sparingly and justified?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `try?` (which converts errors to nil) and `try!` (which crashes on error) are used sparingly, with comments justifying why error details are intentionally discarded or why failure is impossible.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `try?` silently swallows error information, making debugging difficult. `try!` crashes on failure. Both should be rare — most throwing calls should use `do-catch` for proper error handling.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for `try?` and `try!` in the diff
- Verify each `try?` has a comment explaining why the error is intentionally ignored
- Check that `try!` is only used where failure is provably impossible
baseContent.exampleComment: This `try?` swallows the error from `saveToDatabase()`. If the save fails, the user loses data with no feedback. Could we use `do-catch` and show an error alert?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `do-catch` by default — reserve `try?` for intentionally ignored errors and `try!` for provably safe calls.

items.text: Are `Result` types used for completion-handler-based async error handling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Completion handlers use `Result<Success, Failure>` to express success and failure as a single, type-safe value, rather than separate `(data, error)` parameters.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The old `(data?, error?)` pattern allows four states (both nil, both non-nil, either one) when only two are valid. `Result` makes exactly two states possible: `.success` or `.failure`.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check completion handlers for `(Data?, Error?)` dual-optional patterns
- Verify `Result<T, Error>` is used instead
- Look for call sites that don't handle all combinations of the dual-optional
baseContent.exampleComment: This completion handler takes `(User?, Error?)` which allows both to be nil (a state that shouldn't exist). Using `Result<User, Error>` would make the success/failure states unambiguous.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `Result` in completion handlers to make success and failure states unambiguous and exhaustive.

items.text: Are error messages user-friendly for user-facing errors and detailed for developer logs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error messages shown to users are friendly and actionable ('Unable to save. Please try again.'), while developer-facing errors include technical details (status codes, stack context).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users don't understand 'Error: NSURLErrorDomain code=-1009'. Developer logs need those details. Separating the two audiences ensures users get helpful guidance while developers get debuggable information.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that user-facing error alerts use friendly, non-technical language
- Verify `LocalizedError.errorDescription` provides user-friendly messages
- Check that technical details are logged (not shown to users)
- Look for raw error.localizedDescription shown directly in alerts
baseContent.exampleComment: This alert shows `error.localizedDescription` directly, which for network errors displays 'The Internet connection appears to be offline.' — not bad, but for our custom errors it shows internal enum names. Could we use custom user-facing messages?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Show friendly messages to users, log technical details for developers — never expose raw error internals in the UI.

items.text: Is `fatalError()` / `preconditionFailure()` used only for truly unrecoverable states?
